title: What Managers Do: Planning, Organizing, Leading, Controlling

questions.explanation: The conventional order is Planning, Organizing, Leading, Controlling — and it has a logic: plans create the goals, organizing builds the structure to pursue them, leading supplies the willing human effort, and controlling checks results against the plan, feeding the next planning cycle.

Comparing actual results to a standard and initiating corrective action is the controlling function in its textbook four steps: standard → measure → compare → correct. The coaching she schedules is the corrective action; the activity is anchored in measurement against plan, which is what distinguishes controlling from leading.

Transmitting the unit's information outward to external audiences is the spokesperson role (informational cluster). Figurehead is ceremonial duty; disturbance handler reacts to crises; monitor takes information IN. The direction of information flow — inward (monitor), within (disseminator), outward (spokesperson) — is what separates the three informational roles.

Katz's scheme: technical skill dominates at first-line level and fades with altitude; conceptual skill (seeing the organization whole) grows to dominate at the top; and human skill stays roughly constant in importance at every level — the empirical surprise, and the reason people skills are treated as the core managerial competency.

Percent variance = (actual − standard)/standard × 100 = (232 − 250)/250 = −18/250 = −7.2%, so the magnitude is 7.2%. Against a ±5% tolerance band this breaches the threshold, so management by exception says investigate — the variance is the trigger, the diagnosis (one-off vs. systemic cause) determines the corrective action.

Effectiveness is goal attainment (doing the right things): every feature ships on time, so the startup is effective. Efficiency is output relative to resources consumed (doing things right): burning twice the cash of peers for the same output is inefficient. Meeting goals never by itself establishes efficiency — that requires comparing inputs.

Labor productivity = output ÷ labor input = 5,400 units / 450 hours = 12 units per labor-hour. Efficiency measures always normalize output by input — raw output comparisons reward being big, not being well managed.

Communicating the why of a change, coaching individuals through anxiety, and recognizing wins are the leading function — securing willing effort through motivation and communication. No standard is being measured (controlling), no structure designed (organizing), no goals set (planning); the work runs entirely through people.
